title: Project Life Cycles & Process Groups
summary: A project is a temporary endeavor producing a unique result. PMI structures every project around five process groups (Initiating, Planning, Executing, Monitoring & Controlling, Closing) that run inside one or more life-cycle phases.
whyItMatters: Half of fundamentals questions test whether you can place a process or document in the right group. Get the mental map right and the rest follows.
keyPoints: Project = temporary + unique. Operations = ongoing + repeatable.
Process groups are NOT phases — phases are how the WORK is organized; process groups are how the MANAGEMENT is organized.
Initiating: Develop Project Charter + Identify Stakeholders.
Closing: archive records, release resources, formal acceptance.
Validate Scope lives in Monitoring & Controlling (not Closing — common trap).
Program = related projects coordinated for benefits. Portfolio = projects/programs aligned to strategy.
commonMistakes: Calling phases 'process groups' or vice versa.
Placing Validate Scope in Closing.
Confusing a portfolio with a program (programs are related; portfolios may not be).
reference: Process Groups Practice Guide, Sec. 1.4; PMBOK Guide 7th Ed., Sec. 2.3

title: Project Management Planning
summary: Planning translates the charter into actionable baselines (scope, schedule, cost) and subsidiary plans. The output is the integrated project management plan — the reference against which all later performance is measured.
whyItMatters: Most situational questions resolve by asking 'what does the plan say?' Knowing what's IN the plan and what isn't is the test.
keyPoints: Baselines = approved versions of scope, schedule, and cost. Together they form the Performance Measurement Baseline (PMB).
Subsidiary plans: scope, schedule, cost, quality, risk, communications, procurement, resource, stakeholder.
Risk register tracks identified risks + responses + owners. Issue log tracks problems that have already occurred.
Stakeholder register: identity, role, influence, interest, comm prefs.
Milestones have zero duration. Activities consume resources over time.
Project plan ≠ product plan. Project plan ends at closure; product plan spans the product's useful life.
commonMistakes: Treating the risk register and issue log as interchangeable.
Putting the SOW in the project management plan (SOW is a procurement document).
Calling milestones 'tasks'.
reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Ed., Sec. 4.5; Process Groups Practice Guide Sec. 3

title: Roles & Responsibilities
summary: Project manager runs the project. Sponsor funds and champions it. Team executes. Stakeholders are anyone affected by it. Organizational structure (functional / matrix / projectized) decides who has authority over what.
whyItMatters: Many questions hinge on 'whose decision is this?' Get the role boundaries right and you can eliminate two distractors instantly.
keyPoints: Sponsor: funds, champions, removes high-level obstacles, signs the charter.
Project Manager: integrates work, manages stakeholders, owns the schedule and budget.
Functional manager: owns the department's resources.
Strong matrix → PM has high authority. Weak matrix → functional manager does.
Stakeholder grid: Power × Interest → Manage closely / Keep satisfied / Keep informed / Monitor.
Salience model adds Urgency: Definitive = power + legitimacy + urgency.
commonMistakes: Letting the PM 'approve' major scope changes without integrated change control.
Putting a senior exec with low interest in 'Manage closely' (should be 'Keep satisfied').
Confusing the sponsor's role with the PM's.
reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Ed., Sec. 2.4, 2.5; Business Analysis for Practitioners Sec. 2.3

title: Executing Strategies — Communication, Risk, Leadership
summary: Execution is where the plan meets reality. Three competencies dominate the test: communicating with tailored channels, managing risks proactively, and leading the team through Tuckman's stages.
whyItMatters: The hardest scenario questions in Domain 1 are about leadership and communication — there is no 'lookup' answer, only the most-appropriate behavior.
keyPoints: Communication channels = n(n−1)/2. Memorize.
Conflict techniques: Collaborate (best, long-term), Compromise, Smooth, Force, Withdraw.
Risk responses (threats): Avoid, Transfer, Mitigate, Accept. Opportunities: Exploit, Share, Enhance, Accept.
EMV = probability × impact. Add for net expected value.
Tuckman: Forming → Storming → Norming → Performing → Adjourning.
Sources of power: Legitimate, Reward, Coercive, Expert, Referent. Servant leadership is the dominant agile style.
commonMistakes: Picking 'Force' or 'Withdraw' as 'best' — they're worst-long-term answers.
Confusing Mitigate (reduce) with Avoid (eliminate via plan change).
Treating Transfer as 'risk goes away' — the impact moves to a third party but doesn't vanish.

title: Problem-Solving Tools & Techniques
summary: Effective meetings, surfacing blockers, and root-cause analysis. PMI tests whether you treat symptoms or causes — always favor the cause.
whyItMatters: A staple HARD question is: 'Team reports symptoms X, Y, Z. What should the PM do first?' The answer is almost always 'identify the root cause via [fishbone / 5 Whys / Pareto].'
keyPoints: Effective meetings: clear agenda, decisions captured, action items with owners + dates, pre-reads.
Communication blockers: noise, time zones, language, hierarchy, mis-coded messages. Resolutions: shared sources of truth, async decision logs, layered docs.
Root cause tools: Ishikawa (fishbone), 5 Whys, Pareto chart (80/20).
Distinguish a problem from a problem-to-be-solved — symptoms vs cause.
Decision log + lessons learned register turn problems into reusable knowledge.
commonMistakes: Picking the 'easiest symptom to address' instead of root cause.
Escalating to the sponsor before doing any analysis.
Cancelling all meetings 'because they're ineffective' — tightening the agenda is the right move.
reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Ed., Sec. 4.4.5; Process Groups Practice Guide Sec. 5

title: When to Use Predictive (Waterfall)
summary: Predictive suits stable, well-defined scope with strict regulatory or contractual constraints. Plan in detail up front, then execute against baselines.
whyItMatters: Several MOCK questions ask you to choose an approach. Recognize the predictive signals (fixed price, regulated, deliverables fully scoped) and ignore the agile distractors.
keyPoints: Predictive signals: stable scope, fixed-price contract, regulatory rigor, low tolerance for change.
All detailed requirements are locked up front, before significant build.
Baselines (scope/schedule/cost) are the reference; changes require integrated change control.
Performance is measured via Earned Value (EVM).
Hybrid is valid: predictive for regulated portions, agile for evolving portions.
commonMistakes: Picking pure predictive when learning value matters (e.g., a new mobile app inside a regulated hardware project — that part should be agile).
Forgetting that fixed-price contracts strongly favor predictive.
reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Ed., Sec. 2.3.3; Agile Practice Guide Sec. 3

title: Plan Schedule — CPM, Estimating, Compression
summary: Build a network diagram, find the critical path (longest), compute floats, estimate with three-point or PERT, and compress with crashing or fast tracking when needed.
whyItMatters: Pure-calculation questions almost always come from this concept. Memorize the formulas — they're free points.
keyPoints: Critical path = longest path. Determines minimum project duration.
Total Float = LS − ES = LF − EF. Free Float = slip without delaying the next activity.
Dependencies: FS (most common), SS, FF, SF (rare).
Lead = accelerate successor. Lag = mandatory delay.
Three-point (triangular): (O + M + P) / 3. PERT (beta): (O + 4M + P) / 6.
Crashing: add resources → raises cost. Fast tracking: parallelize → raises risk.
Resource leveling can EXTEND the schedule and change the critical path. Smoothing works within float and does NOT change the CP.
commonMistakes: Mixing up triangular and PERT formulas. Read the prompt carefully — 'PERT' means /6, 'triangular' means /3.
Calling the shortest path 'critical' (it's the longest).
Thinking fast tracking raises cost (it raises risk).

title: Project Controls — EVM & Change Control
summary: Earned Value Management measures cost and schedule performance against the baseline using indices (CPI, SPI) and forecasts (EAC, ETC). Integrated change control governs how baselines are updated.
whyItMatters: Every MOCK has 3-5 EVM calculations. Each one is worth a point — and the math is mechanical.
keyPoints: CV = EV − AC.  SV = EV − PV.
CPI = EV / AC. SPI = EV / PV. <1 = bad.
EAC = BAC / CPI (typical variance). ETC = EAC − AC.
CPI > 1 + SPI < 1 usually means under-resourced (fewer people working = less cost, less progress).
Approved scope changes → update the cost baseline; management reserve is sponsor-controlled.
PMB integrates scope + schedule + cost baselines.
commonMistakes: Inverting CPI / SPI (it's EV in the numerator both times).
Multiplying instead of dividing in EAC.
Updating the schedule baseline without going through integrated change control.
reference: PMBOK Guide 7th Ed., Sec. 4.6.6; Process Groups Practice Guide Sec. 5.6

title: When to Use Adaptive (Agile)
summary: Agile fits when requirements are evolving, feedback loops are short, and the customer needs working product frequently. The opposite signals favor predictive.
whyItMatters: Approach-selection questions are easy points if you recognize the situational tells. Don't overthink — agile = changing requirements + frequent feedback.
keyPoints: Strong agile signals: changing requirements, frequent demos, uncertain market fit, ability to deliver in increments.
Strong predictive signals: regulated, fixed-price, fully-scoped, change-averse customer.
Hybrid is common: regulated chunks predictive, evolving software chunks agile, joined by a shared roadmap.
Agile mindset > any specific framework: deliver value, learn, adapt.
commonMistakes: Picking pure agile for a hardware compliance project.
Picking pure predictive for a product where customer learning is the whole point.
reference: Agile Practice Guide, Sec. 3; PMBOK Guide 7th Ed., Sec. 2.3

title: Agile Project Management — Manifesto, Scrum Roles
summary: Four manifesto values + 12 principles + three Scrum roles (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Dev Team) form the backbone of every agile question.
whyItMatters: Half the agile bank tests role responsibilities. Knowing exactly what each Scrum role does — and DOES NOT do — eliminates wrong answers fast.
keyPoints: Manifesto values (LHS over RHS): Individuals/interactions over processes/tools, Working software over comprehensive docs, Customer collaboration over contract negotiation, Responding to change over following a plan.
Product Owner: orders the backlog, maximizes value, accepts work.
Scrum Master: servant leader, removes impediments, defends the process. NOT a task assigner.
Dev Team: self-organizing, cross-functional, builds the increment to DoD.
Servant leadership = put team's growth/well-being first.
commonMistakes: Treating the SM as a project manager (assigning tasks, setting deadlines).
Having the SM accept work — that's the PO's job.
Inverting the manifesto pairs (e.g., 'following a plan over responding to change').

title: Plan Project Iterations — Scrum Events
summary: Sprints are time-boxed, with five recurring events: planning, daily, review, retrospective, and the sprint itself.
whyItMatters: Event-purpose questions are common. Pin each event to its inspect-and-adapt target (work, product, or process).
keyPoints: Sprint planning: set the sprint goal + select backlog items + plan the first few days.
Daily Scrum: 15 min, plan the next 24 hours, surface impediments. Inspect progress toward sprint goal.
Sprint review: stakeholders inspect the INCREMENT, adapt the backlog.
Retrospective: team inspects its own PROCESS, picks 1–3 improvements with owners.
Mid-sprint scope additions: defer, or surface the trade-off (drop equivalent work).
Planning poker: outliers explain, then re-vote. Don't average.
commonMistakes: Reviewing process at the Review (that's the Retro).
Accepting any mid-sprint scope add without a trade-off.
Averaging planning-poker estimates instead of discussing outliers.

title: Adaptive Project Controls — Velocity, Burn Charts
summary: Agile teams measure progress with empirical metrics: velocity (work per sprint), burn-down (remaining work), burn-up (completed + total scope), lead time, cycle time.
whyItMatters: Forecasting and metric-interpretation questions reward picking the metric that fits the question's intent.
keyPoints: Velocity = average completed story points per sprint. Use the average + a range, not the peak.
Burn-down: above the ideal line = behind plan. Hides scope changes.
Burn-up: shows completed work AND total scope on one chart. Best for visible scope change.
Lead time = full time from request to delivery. Cycle time = active work time only.
Cumulative flow diagram surfaces bottlenecks (queues growing).
Re-plan signals: sustained velocity change, accepted scope change, priority shifts.
commonMistakes: Using peak velocity to forecast — overcommits.
Picking burn-down for a project with constant scope changes (use burn-up).
Treating cycle time and lead time as the same thing.

title: Components of an Adaptive Plan — Backlog, Stories, MVP
summary: Adaptive plans live in a prioritized backlog of user stories, refined just-in-time, with a Definition of Done and a Minimum Viable Product as the early-delivery target.
whyItMatters: Question prompts that include 'backlog', 'INVEST', 'DoD', or 'MVP' are testing a few well-defined rules. Memorize the rules and you score.
keyPoints: Story template: 'As a <role>, I want <feature>, so that <benefit>.'
INVEST: Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable.
Acceptance Criteria are story-specific and defined BEFORE development.
Definition of Done is team-wide and applies to every item.
MVP = smallest version that delivers value and enables learning. NOT a prototype, NOT the full product.
commonMistakes: Confusing acceptance criteria (story-level) with DoD (team-wide).
Writing 'As a developer, I want…' — that's a task, not a user story.
Calling a never-released prototype an MVP.

title: Task Management — Kanban & Flow
summary: Kanban visualizes work, limits WIP, and manages flow. WIP limits exist to surface bottlenecks — when you hit one, stop starting and start finishing.
whyItMatters: Flow-and-bottleneck questions are dense in Domain 3. The fix is almost never 'add more people' or 'raise the WIP limit'.
keyPoints: Core Kanban practices: visualize workflow, limit WIP, manage flow, make policies explicit, feedback loops, improve.
When WIP is over the limit: stop starting, start finishing. Don't raise the limit.
Bottleneck = queue building up before a stage. Help downstream finish; reduce upstream WIP.
Throughput = items completed per period. Differs from velocity (story points).
Pull, not push: team members pull work as capacity opens.
commonMistakes: Raising WIP limit to 'unblock' a stuck flow.
Adding people to fix a bottleneck without first reducing upstream WIP.
Switching framework (Scrum/Kanban) instead of fixing the actual flow problem.

title: BA Roles & Responsibilities
summary: The BA elicits, analyzes, and validates requirements. Where no dedicated BA exists, PM + PO + SMEs share the work. The role boundary matters — questions test who owns what.
whyItMatters: Several scenarios test 'who should do this?'. Knowing where the BA stops and the PM starts removes two distractors.
keyPoints: BA: elicits, analyzes, validates requirements; manages requirements lifecycle.
PM: integrates work, manages schedule/budget/team. Does NOT own requirements.
When no BA: PM + PO + SMEs share. The work cannot be skipped.
BA's first move when joining: identify stakeholders + understand the problem space.
Resolve PM/BA role conflicts through direct, private conversation first; escalate only if needed.
commonMistakes: Letting the PM make requirement-level decisions without the BA/SMEs.
Skipping BA work when there's no dedicated BA — instead, share it explicitly.
Escalating role conflicts to the sponsor before having the peer conversation.

title: Stakeholder Communication
summary: Tailor channel, detail, and formality to each stakeholder. Use layered documents for mixed audiences, decision logs to make agreements stick, and direct conversation to repair relationships.
whyItMatters: BA scenarios often ask 'what should the BA do FIRST?' The correct answer almost always involves listening + tailoring before broadcasting.
keyPoints: Tailor by audience: executives → outcomes, devs → specs, users → benefits.
Layered docs: exec summary up top, detailed annexes below. One source of truth.
Decisions-in-writing policy beats verbal-only when stakeholders deny later.
Re-engagement: private listening session FIRST, not public apology.
Salience model: Definitive stakeholders (Power + Legitimacy + Urgency) get the highest engagement.
commonMistakes: Sending the same dense report to every stakeholder.
Public apology before a private listening session.
Treating verbal agreement as durable record.

title: Gathering Requirements
summary: Pick the elicitation technique that fits the situation, prioritize with MoSCoW, classify by type (business, stakeholder, functional, non-functional, transition), and trace everything via the RTM.
whyItMatters: Domain 4 is the largest by question count. Mastering elicitation choice + classification + MoSCoW alone unlocks ~15 questions.
keyPoints: Elicitation: Interview (1:1), Workshop (group alignment), Document analysis (existing artifacts), Observation (tacit knowledge), Prototyping (validate UI before commit).
Combine techniques to triangulate; one is rarely enough.
MoSCoW: Must / Should / Could / Won't (this time).
Requirement types: Business (goals), Stakeholder (specific need), Functional (what it does), Non-functional (qualities), Transition (move from current to future, temporary).
RTM links each requirement forward (design/test/delivery) and backward (source).
When a stakeholder describes solutions, probe for the underlying need before capturing.
commonMistakes: Using a workshop for tacit knowledge (observation is better).
Treating Should-have as Must-have (erodes prioritization).
Skipping RTM for 'speed' — defects later cost more.

title: Product Roadmaps
summary: Roadmaps communicate direction and approximate timing — they are NOT detailed plans. The further out, the less specific.
whyItMatters: PMI questions punish over-commitment on roadmaps. The right answer is usually 'theme/horizon-based with confidence ranges,' not 'exact dates for the year'.
keyPoints: Roadmap = direction + themes + approximate milestones, NOT a sprint plan.
Detailed dates beyond 1–2 quarters on adaptive products → over-commitment.
Use now/next/later or quarter-themed formats with confidence levels.
Roadmap audience: executives + customers. Detail level should match.
Update cadence: quarterly review minimum, more often if priorities shift.
commonMistakes: Committing exact dates 12 months out on an adaptive product.
Mixing sprint plans into the roadmap (wrong altitude).
Treating the roadmap as a budget document.

title: Methodology's Influence on BA
summary: Predictive locks requirements up front. Adaptive uses a progressively elaborated backlog. Hybrid baselines stable scope and elaborates volatile scope iteratively.
whyItMatters: A few hard questions test whether you understand that the BA's PRACTICES change with methodology — but the BA's RESPONSIBILITIES don't.
keyPoints: Predictive: detailed requirements signed off in planning. Changes via integrated change control.
Adaptive: backlog of user stories, refined just-in-time, prioritized continuously.
Hybrid: baseline + progressively elaborate, with DoR/DoD for each backlog item.
Traceability matters in ALL methodologies — its form changes, its purpose doesn't.
Skipping BA on agile portions is a failure pattern — share it across PO/SMEs instead.
commonMistakes: Documenting everything up front on an adaptive project.
Skipping BA entirely because 'the team is agile'.
Treating traceability as optional when methodology changes.

title: Validate Requirements Through Delivery
summary: Validation = right product (meets need). Verification = product right (meets specs). Acceptance criteria are written BEFORE development, and UAT walks through real-world scenarios with users.
whyItMatters: The validation-vs-verification distinction is one of the most frequently tested BA concepts. Lock it.
keyPoints: Validation = needs. Verification = specs. (V_alidation = V_alue.)
Acceptance criteria are testable, defined up front, and aligned to the user need.
Pass AC but fail user need → validation failure, not verification failure.
UAT with scenario walkthroughs validates real-world fit.
Production defects tracing to ambiguity → add AC + traceability + peer review on requirements.
commonMistakes: Writing acceptance criteria AFTER development.
Confusing 'passed tests' with 'meets the need'.
Demanding more testing as the fix when the root cause is ambiguous requirements.
